skill.name: web-fetch
skill.description: Fetches HTTP and HTTPS pages, extracts the main content, and converts HTML into cleaner Markdown for reference and documentation workflows.
skill.summary: Fetch web pages and convert main HTML content into clean Markdown.
skill.author: 0xBigBoss

security_audit.summary: The static high-risk weak-crypto findings are false positives caused by Markdown text and an aria-label string check, with no evidence of cryptographic logic. The real risks are intentional arbitrary URL fetching and documented shell command usage, which can expose internal network resources or command-injection hazards if used with untrusted URLs.

medium_findings.title: Arbitrary URL Fetch Can Reach Sensitive Network Targets
medium_findings.description: The script reads a URL from the command line and passes it directly to fetch without scheme, host, or private-network restrictions. This is core functionality, but it can become SSRF-like access to internal services or metadata endpoints when run in a privileged environment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The data flow from process.argv[2] to fetch(url) is direct and visible. The risk depends on runtime network position, so this is elevated but not evidence of malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Shell Command Workflow Requires Careful URL Quoting
medium_findings.description: The instructions recommend curl pipelines where a URL placeholder is inserted into shell commands. If an agent substitutes an untrusted URL without robust quoting, shell metacharacters in the URL could alter the command.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documented commands are legitimate examples, but they are shell workflows with user-controlled URL input. The exploitability depends on how the assistant or user substitutes the URL.

low_findings.title: Hidden Claude Skill Path Used For Setup
low_findings.description: The documentation references ~/.claude/skills/web-fetch for running and installing the local skill. This is hidden-directory access, but no evidence shows arbitrary hidden-file reads, secret access, or exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced path is limited to the skill installation directory. The static filesystem warning is real as a capability indicator but low severity in this context.

low_findings.title: Static Backtick And Weak-Crypto Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The Ruby backtick execution alerts in fetch.ts are JavaScript template literals that build Markdown code fences or status text. The weak-crypto alert points to a Copy label check, not a cryptographic algorithm.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line review shows Markdown fences, shell examples, or ordinary string interpolation rather than Ruby execution or crypto use. No execution primitive appears in fetch.ts beyond the intended fetch call.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unrestricted Network Request From CLI Argument
dangerous_patterns.description: The script accepts a command-line URL and fetches it directly. Publication should warn users not to fetch untrusted internal, localhost, link-local, or metadata-service URLs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The source and sink are both explicit in adjacent lines. The pattern is common in web fetchers but still security-sensitive.

dangerous_patterns.title: Documented Shell Pipelines With URL Placeholders
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill teaches curl and pipeline commands for arbitrary URLs. These commands should be generated with strict argument quoting and should not include URL text copied from untrusted pages.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shell commands are plainly documented and useful. The danger is contextual, based on how URL values are substituted before execution.

content.user_title: Fetch Web Pages as Clean Markdown
content.value_statement: Web pages often include navigation, scripts, and layout noise that make source material hard to review. This skill fetches page content and converts the useful HTML into cleaner Markdown for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.
actual_capabilities: Fetches HTTP and HTTPS page content from a provided URL.
Converts selected HTML content into Markdown with fenced code blocks.
Uses documented CSS selectors for Anthropic docs, MDN, GitHub docs, and generic articles.
Falls back to a Bun script that selects the richest article, main, or content element.
Removes common navigation, sidebar, header, footer, script, and style elements.
Prints the page title and cleaned Markdown output for review.
limitations: Does not render JavaScript-only pages or browser-managed application state.
May select the wrong content area on pages with several article-like sections.
Requires curl, html2markdown, or Bun dependencies depending on the chosen workflow.
Should not be used on private, localhost, link-local, or metadata-service URLs without review.

use_cases.title: Review API Documentation
use_cases.description: Fetch a documentation page and convert the central content into Markdown for faster analysis.

use_cases.title: Collect Article References
use_cases.description: Extract the main body of an article while removing navigation and footer content.

use_cases.title: Prepare Source Material For Coding Tasks
use_cases.description: Turn web reference pages into clean Markdown that an assistant can summarize or compare.

prompt_templates.title: Fetch A Documentation Page
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a known documentation URL.
prompt_templates.prompt: Fetch this documentation page and return the main content as clean Markdown: <url>

prompt_templates.title: Extract An Article
prompt_templates.prompt: Fetch this article, remove navigation and footer content, and summarize the useful Markdown sections: <url>

prompt_templates.title: Use A Specific Selector
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request when the main content selector is known.
prompt_templates.prompt: Fetch <url> using the selector <selector>, convert it to Markdown, and preserve code blocks.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Multiple References
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced research across several trusted pages.
prompt_templates.prompt: Fetch these trusted documentation URLs, convert each main content area to Markdown, and compare the API differences: <url list>

output_examples.input: Fetch an MDN article about a web API.
output_examples.output: A Markdown document with the page title, headings, explanation text, and preserved examples from the article body.

output_examples.input: Fetch a GitHub documentation page with navigation excluded.
output_examples.output: A concise Markdown version of the documentation page without sidebar links or repeated navigation labels.

output_examples.input: Fetch an unknown article page with the Bun fallback.
output_examples.output: A cleaned Markdown page selected from the richest article, main, or content container found in the HTML.

best_practices: Use trusted HTTP or HTTPS URLs and avoid private network addresses.
Choose a specific CSS selector when the page structure is known.
Review the extracted Markdown before relying on it for technical decisions.
anti_patterns: Do not fetch URLs copied from untrusted text without checking the full address.
Do not use this skill for pages that require browser login, JavaScript rendering, or interactive state.
Do not run shell examples with unquoted or dynamically generated URL strings.

faq.question: What does this skill fetch?
faq.answer: It fetches HTML pages from provided HTTP or HTTPS URLs and converts the useful content into Markdown.

faq.question: Does it work with JavaScript-rendered pages?
faq.answer: No. It reads the server response and does not run a browser or render client-side JavaScript.

faq.question: Can it preserve code blocks?
faq.answer: Yes. The Bun script includes handling for preformatted code blocks and fenced Markdown output.

faq.question: Why are CSS selectors useful?
faq.answer: Selectors limit extraction to the main content area and reduce navigation, sidebar, and footer noise.

faq.question: Is it safe to fetch any URL?
faq.answer: No. Avoid private, localhost, link-local, metadata-service, and untrusted URLs unless you have reviewed the risk.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
